elements.sent-2411-001: Surgical instrument segmentation (SIS) is an essential task in computer-assisted surgeries, with deep learning-based research improving accuracy in complex environments.
elements.sent-2411-002: Recently, text-promptable segmentation methods have been introduced to generate masks based on text prompts describing target objects.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, these methods assume that the object described by a given text prompt exists in the scene.
elements.sent-2411-004: This results in mask generation whenever a related text prompt is provided, even if the object is absent from the image.
elements.sent-2411-005: Existing methods handle this by using prompts only for objects known to be present in the image, which introduces inaccessible information in a vision-based method setting and results in unfair comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-006: For fair comparison, we redefine existing text-promptable SIS settings to robust conditions, called Robust text-promptable SIS (R-SIS), designed to forward prompts of all classes and determine the existence of an object from a given text prompt for the fair comparison.
elements.sent-2411-007: Furthermore, we propose a novel framework, Robust Surgical Instrument Segmentation (RoSIS), which combines visual and language features for promptable segmentation in the R-SIS setting.
elements.sent-2411-008: RoSIS employs an encoder-decoder architecture with a Multi-Modal Fusion Block (MMFB) and a Selective Gate Block (SGB) to achieve balanced integration of vision and language features.
elements.sent-2411-009: Additionally, we introduce an iterative inference strategy that refines segmentation masks in two steps: an initial pass using name-based prompts, followed by a refinement step using location prompts.
elements.sent-2411-010: Experiments on various datasets and settings demonstrate that RoSIS outperforms existing vision-based and promptable methods under robust conditions.
elements.sent-2411-011: Surgical Instrument Segmentation (SIS) \cite{shvets2018automatic has gained attention as a fundamental technology required to assist surgeons in this surgical vision environment.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Various vision-based deep learning methods \cite{iglovikov2018ternausnet, jin2019incorporating, zhao2020learning, gonzalez2020isinet, baby2023forks, ayobi2023matis have achieved impressive performance.}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, due to the characteristics of SIS, these methods often rely on datasets of limited scale, resulting in insufficient performance, and they lack the capability in terms of human-robot interaction for robot-assisted surgeries.
elements.sent-2411-014: Recently, language and vision-language model-based methods \cite{zhou2023text, yue2023part have been developed to overcome these challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-015: They introduced a new approach to SIS called text-promptable surgical instrument segmentation \cite{zhou2023text based on Referring Image Segmentation (RIS) \cite{yang2022lavt, yu2018mattnet .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: RIS generates an object mask based on a given text description.
elements.sent-2411-017: However, RIS methods typically assume that the object described by the text exists within the image.
elements.sent-2411-018: When an incorrect or unrelated text is provided, the model produces a mismatched mask.
elements.sent-2411-019: To address this issue, Generalized Referring Expression Segmentation (GRES) \cite{liu2023gres and Robust Referring Image Segmentation (R-RIS) \cite{wu2024towards have been proposed.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: These methods first classify whether the object described by the text actually exists in the image.
elements.sent-2411-021: If the object is present, they proceed to predict the mask.
elements.sent-2411-022: A similar issue arises in RIS-based text-promptable SIS methods.
elements.sent-2411-023: These methods define a text sentence by utilizing the instrument's name along with a detailed description generated from the large language model such as GPT-4 \cite{achiam2023gpt .}
elements.sent-2411-024: They then predict a mask that corresponds to the given instrument prompt.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, even when a text prompt for an instrument that is not present in the image is provided, the model still produces an incorrect mask.
elements.sent-2411-026: Existing methods handle this by first identifying the instruments in the image and then providing prompts only for those that are detected.
elements.sent-2411-027: However, it utilizes inaccessible information (the existence of an object) in the evaluation phase, causing an unfair comparison with vision-based models.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figure/figure1.pdf} \caption{Comparison of surgical instrument segmentation method: (1) Vision-based segmentation model only uses the image and generates all classes of segmentation maps; (2) Previous promptable segmentation model uses ``not accessible information" to identify categories present in the image and generates segmentation maps only for those categories. This means they are led to settings without robust conditions; (3) Our robust promptable segmentation model uses prompts for all classes, generating segmentation maps for all categories under robust conditions.} \label{fig:fig1}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figure/figure2.pdf} \caption{Performance comparison on the EndoVis2018 dataset under with and without robust conditions. Existing models exhibit significant performance degradation, with dramatic drops in both Challenge IoU (Ch IoU) and ISI IoU. In contrast, our robust promptable segmentation model demonstrates minimal performance degradation across both metrics and outperforms the vision-based SOTA method (ISINet).} \label{fig:fig2}
elements.sent-2411-028: Accordingly, inspired by the R-RIS \cite{wu2024towards approach, we redefine the text-promptable SIS under robust conditions as called Robust text-promptable Surgical Instrument Segmentation (R-SIS).}
elements.sent-2411-029: As illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:fig1 , previous promptable SIS models \cite{zhou2023text, yue2024surgicalsam, yue2023part use prompts only for categories present within the image, which is inaccessible information in the vision-based models.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: They generate segmentation maps for a subset of classes because they only use prompts for objects in the image.
elements.sent-2411-031: Unlike previous approaches, R-SIS ensures a fair comparison by incorporating text prompts for all categories within the dataset, regardless of their presence in the image.
elements.sent-2411-032: To achieve this robustness, R-SIS employs negative prompts for non-existing objects and positive prompts for existing objects in training, enabling the model to classify each object's presence or absence effectively.
elements.sent-2411-033: This strategy avoids false positives and improves accuracy in diverse and realistic surgical scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-034: To support the development and evaluation of R-SIS, we build the dataset for R-SIS using the EndoVis2017 \cite{allan20192017 and EndoVis2018 \cite{allan20202018 datasets.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: This adapted dataset includes comprehensive prompts for R-SIS, facilitating more reliable training and evaluation under robust conditions.
elements.sent-2411-036: Previous promptable SIS methods assume the presence of an object matching the given text, which results in increased false positives in the R-SIS environment.
elements.sent-2411-037: As shown in Fig. \ref{fig:fig2 , previous methods suffer from significant performance degradation when evaluated under robust conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-038: There are even cases where the performance is lower than that of vision-based methods that do not use language information.
elements.sent-2411-039: To address this limitation, we propose a novel framework designed to perform well in robust conditions.
elements.sent-2411-040: Our model, named Robust Surgical Instrument Segmentation (RoSIS), includes two key modules: the Multi-Modal Fusion Block (MMFB) and the Selective Gate Block (SGB).
elements.sent-2411-041: The MMFB effectively integrates visual and language features to enhance contextual understanding with language tokens, while the SGB dynamically adjusts the balance of visual and fused features.
elements.sent-2411-042: We also propose the iterative inference to generate more accurate segmentation maps at test time.
elements.sent-2411-043: Iterative inference produces a refined map through two iterations: the first iteration uses prompts based on the object name and a generative model, while the second iteration incorporates location prompts generated using the initial map.
elements.sent-2411-044: This architecture and strategy enable RoSIS to achieve stable and accurate segmentation across a broad range of surgical conditions.
elements.sent-2411-045: Our model demonstrates competitive performance on the EndoVis2017 and EndoVis2018, achieving powerful results to vision-based models under robust evaluation conditions.
elements.sent-2411-046: To ensure this robustness, we carefully revisit our experimental process, allowing for precise benchmarking.
elements.sent-2411-047: Additionally, we conduct comparisons with existing promptable methods under the R-RIS setting to further validate our approach.
elements.sent-2411-048: This thorough evaluation shows the robustness and effectiveness of our model, highlighting its ability to maintain high accuracy and reliability, even when directly compared with vision-based and promptable segmentation methods.
elements.sent-2411-049: Surgical instrument segmentation (SIS) involves predicting the pixel-wise regions of instruments in surgical images.
elements.sent-2411-050: Early research \cite{iglovikov2018ternausnet, gonzalez2020isinet, zhao2022trasetr, ayobi2023matis, baby2023forks in this field applied image-based segmentation methods to address SIS.}
elements.sent-2411-051: TernausNet \cite{iglovikov2018ternausnet marked the beginning of deep learning-based approaches by introducing a modified U-Net \cite{ronneberger2015u architecture based semantic segmentation framework with pre-trained VGG11/VGG16 encoders and skip connections for precise location.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: ISINet \cite{gonzalez2020isinet , employing a Mask R-CNN \cite{he2017mask architecture, incorporates a novel temporal consistency module to track and correct instrument predictions across video frames.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: TraSeTR \cite{zhao2022trasetr utilizes a Track-to-Segment Transformer architecture, combining query embeddings with contrastive learning to ensure robust tracking and segmentation across video frames.}
elements.sent-2411-054: MATIS \cite{ayobi2023matis , a video-based SIS method, leverages a two-stage structure with Mask2Former \cite{cheng2022masked to generate and classify region proposals using masked and deformable attention and video transformers to maintain temporal consistency.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: S3Net \cite{baby2023forks builds on Mask R-CNN \cite{he2017mask with a three-stage architecture, adding a Multi-Scale Mask-Attended classifier to refine class predictions based on mask-attended features, improving instance segmentation of surgical instruments.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Recently, with the development of segmentation techniques that utilize information beyond vision, promptable segmentation models that use external prompts, such as text or prototypes, have been proposed for SIS.
elements.sent-2411-057: SurgicalSAM \cite{yue2024surgicalsam proposes an efficiently fine-tuning method for Segment Anything Model (SAM) \cite{kirillov2023segment .}}
elements.sent-2411-058: SAM is a segmentation model using prompts (points, bounding boxes, or text inputs) from prompt encoder.
elements.sent-2411-059: Since SAM was originally designed for natural domain image segmentation, SurgicalSAM adapts SAM for the surgical domain, making it suitable for SIS.
elements.sent-2411-060: Additionally, SurgicalSAM introduces a prototype-based class prompt encoder that generates prompt embeddings for each class, which are then used as SAM prompts.
elements.sent-2411-061: Later, various text-promptable SIS methods were proposed, leveraging vision-language models (VLM) to use text prompts for segmentation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-062: SP-SAM \cite{yue2023part uses SAM \cite{kirillov2023segment as its baseline and incorporates both category-level and part-level text prompts as prompt embeddings.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: TP-SIS \cite{zhou2023text , a method based on CLIP \cite{radford2021learning , utilizes text prompts describing instruments, which are categorized into name-based prompts, CLIP’s prompt templates, and prompts generated from GPT-4 \cite{achiam2023gpt .}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Although these methods have been proposed for text-promptable SIS, they are trained and inferred with the assumption that the object described by the text expression exists within the image.
elements.sent-2411-065: As a result, their performance degrades significantly when a text prompt refers to an object not present in the image.
elements.sent-2411-066: In this paper, we address this issue by adopting a robust referring image segmentation \cite{wu2024towards approach for text-promptable SIS.}
elements.sent-2411-067: We first determine the existence of the object to reduce false positives and handle negative expressions.
elements.sent-2411-068: Furthermore, we resolve the unfair comparisons that occur in existing text-promptable segmentation models, ensuring a fair comparison with vision-based segmentation models.
elements.sent-2411-069: Referring Image Segmentation (RIS) aims to segment the object corresponding to a given text expression.
elements.sent-2411-070: MAttNet \cite{yu2018mattnet designs a language attention network using word embeddings and an LSTM, then compares these with object proposals to generate a mask that matches the expression.}
elements.sent-2411-071: VLT \cite{ding2021vision and EFN \cite{feng2021encoder propose a Bi-GRU language model and a ResNet-based image backbone to generate features fused in the decoder through multi-modal information integration.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: With the development of Vision Transformers (ViT) \cite{dosovitskiy2020image , various methods are applied ViT to RIS tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-073: LAVT \cite{yang2022lavt introduced an early fusion method that combines text features generated by BERT \cite{devlin2018bert with visual features from ViT through pixel-word attention, achieving significant performance improvements.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: However, previous works assumed that each expression must always match an object, which led to the crucial issue of generating incorrect masks for expressions that do not correspond to any object in the image.
elements.sent-2411-075: ReLA \cite{liu2023gres overcomes this by proposing Generalized Referring Expression Segmentation, incorporating empty-target (no target for given text) and multi-target annotations (multiple targets for given text).}
elements.sent-2411-076: RefSegformer \cite{wu2024towards focuses on the empty-target scenario, proposing a robust referring image segmentation approach.}
elements.sent-2411-077: The empty-target problem also arises in existing promptable surgical instrument segmentation methods, and we aim to address this issue in our work.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figure/figure3.pdf} \caption{RoSIS model architecture with (a) overall structure, (b) Multi-Modal Fusion Block (MMFB), and (c) Selective Gate Block (SGB).} \label{fig:fig3}
elements.sent-2411-078: This section presents our approach to robust text-promptable surgical instrument segmentation (R-SIS).
elements.sent-2411-079: Section \ref{sec:sec3.1 formulates the problem for R-SIS, identifying key challenges in promptable segmentation for surgical instruments.}
elements.sent-2411-080: Section \ref{sec:sec3.2 defines the construction of text prompts used for training, detailing the use of descriptive prompts to enhance model understanding.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Section \ref{sec:sec3.3 describes the model architecture, including the encoder-decoder structure and multi-modal fusion components.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Finally, Section \ref{sec:sec3.4 introduces an iterative inference strategy aimed at refining mask accuracy through repeated inference steps.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Surgical instrument segmentation (SIS) is the task of segmenting surgical tools in an endoscopic surgery image \( I \in \mathbb{R ^{H \times W \times 3 \), where \( H \) and \( W \) denote the height and width of the image, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: For text-promptable SIS, we incorporate an image \( I \) and a text prompt \( T \) containing the names or attributes of the surgical tools, making the segmentation task responsive to specific prompts.
elements.sent-2411-085: In traditional vision-based SIS, the model takes only image \( I \) as input and predicts a mask \( M \in \mathbb{R ^{H \times W \times C \) that includes \( C \) classes, each corresponding to a specific surgical instrument.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: However, in text-promptable SIS, both the image \( I \) and a class-specific text prompt \( t_c \) are provided as input for each class \( c \).
elements.sent-2411-087: This enables the model to generate a binary mask \( M_c \in \mathbb{R ^{H \times W \times 1 \) for each class \( c \).}}
elements.sent-2411-088: For the robust text-promptable surgical instrument segmentation (R-RIS), we also generate an existence probability \( p_c \), which classifies the class \( c \) in the image for a given text prompt \( t_c \).
elements.sent-2411-089: In the test phase, we conduct inference across all \( C \) classes for a given image \( I \), resulting in class-specific masks \( M_1, \ldots, M_C \) and existence probabilities \( p_1, \ldots, p_C \).
elements.sent-2411-090: These outputs are then aggregated to the final mask \( M \in \mathbb{R ^{H \times W \times C \), with each pixel in \( M \) representing the class-specific segmentation.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: To ensure R-SIS, we incorporate existence probabilities, allowing our model to assess the presence of each instrument class first.
elements.sent-2411-092: This setup enables to avoidance of producing unnecessary masks for absent classes, reducing false positives, and enhancing segmentation accuracy under the R-SIS setting.
elements.sent-2411-093: By integrating class existence probabilities, our method is evaluated under robust conditions with vision-based models and demonstrates results under practical conditions.
elements.sent-2411-094: There are various ways to create text prompts \( t_c \) for each class \( c \).
elements.sent-2411-095: Following the approach in TP-SIS \cite{zhou2023text , we generate prompts using the class name, such as ``The \{cls name\}" and we utilize GPT-4 \cite{achiam2023gpt to generate detailed prompts describing each class’s visual characteristics.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: This combination provides both general and specific prompts, enabling the model to recognize instruments based on both name and appearance.
elements.sent-2411-097: Since surgical instruments often appear in consistent positions on the screen during surgery, we further enhance the prompts by designing location-based descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-098: To do this, we calculate the center of mass for each instrument's ground truth mask to identify its typical position, creating prompts like ``The \{cls name\} on the \{location\}." For location prompts, we categorize positions into four options: ``left-top," ``left-bottom," ``right-top," and ``right-bottom." During model training, we employ all three types of text prompts—class name prompt, GPT-4 visual prompt, and location prompt—for each class \( c \), ensuring comprehensive contextual information.
elements.sent-2411-099: If training only with positive prompts (describing objects present in the image), the model may develop a bias toward the presence of instruments, potentially reducing generalization \cite{wu2024towards .}
elements.sent-2411-100: To counter this, we also generate negative prompts for classes absent from the image, thus enhancing the classification ability to distinguish between existing and non-existing classes.
elements.sent-2411-101: For absent classes, we create prompts using both the class name and GPT-4 description prompts, as well as a randomly selected location prompt from the four options, to provide a full range of negative examples.
elements.sent-2411-102: We balance the positive and negative prompts corresponding to one image for stable training.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figure/figure4.pdf} \caption{Iterative segmentation process: (Iteration 1) initial segmentation using a general prompt, (Iteration 2) refined segmentation with a location prompt referring to the first predicted map. Then, we combine maps by equation \ref{eq:eq3} for accurate final output.} \label{fig:fig4}
elements.sent-2411-103: We design our Robust Surgical Instrument Segmentation (RoSIS) using an encoder-decoder structure to achieve robust text-promptable surgical instrument segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-104: Inspired by \cite{wu2024towards and \cite{liu2023gres , we add a binary classifier in parallel with the decoder to determine the existence of instruments.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: First, we encode the image \( I \) and text description \( T \) using an image encoder and a text encoder, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-106: The image encoder is based on the Swin Transformer \cite{liu2021swin , which is specialized for dense prediction tasks, while the text encoder uses BERT \cite{devlin2018bert .}}
elements.sent-2411-107: We denote the visual feature from stage \( i \) of the Swin Transformer as \( v_i \in \mathbb{R ^{H_i \times W_i \times C_i \) and the language feature from BERT as \( l \in \mathbb{R ^{T \times C_l \), where \( H_i \) and \( W_i \) are the height and width of the visual feature, \( T \) is the number of words in the text description, and \( C_i \) and \( C_l \) represent the number of channels for the visual and language features, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: We introduce early feature fusion to efficiently integrate vision and language knowledge from the encoder.
elements.sent-2411-109: We aim to enhance semantic understanding and improve feature interactivity through early fusion.
elements.sent-2411-110: For this purpose, we add the Multi-Modal Fusion Block (MMFB) and the Selective Gate Block (SGB) between each stage of the Swin Transformer.
elements.sent-2411-111: Fig. \ref{fig:fig3 (b) shows the architecture of the MMFB, which is designed to integrate visual and language features through a multi-step fusion process, with the fused feature then passed to a gating module.}
elements.sent-2411-112: The fusion process begins by independently projecting the visual and language features into compatible feature spaces.
elements.sent-2411-113: The MMFB contains two Multi-Head Cross Attention (MHCA) modules.
elements.sent-2411-114: In the first MHCA, we adopt language tokens to fuse with the language feature.
elements.sent-2411-115: Unlike \cite{wu2024towards , since we do not use the language features generated during fusion in the decoder, we focus on selecting only the information necessary for fusion with visual features.}
elements.sent-2411-116: Therefore, we refine the language features with one MHCA and subsequently use them in the second MHCA to interact with the visual features.
elements.sent-2411-117: Inspired by \cite{yang2022lavt , after the MMFB, we introduce the SGB to regulate the flow of fused features, ensuring that language features do not dominate the visual information.}
elements.sent-2411-118: The SGB dynamically controls feature importance, allowing the model to maintain a balanced representation of both visual and language features before passing them to the next stage.
elements.sent-2411-119: As shown in Fig. \ref{fig:fig3 (c), the SGB takes the fused feature as input, processes it through a series of lightweight transformations and applies a gating mechanism.}
elements.sent-2411-120: The whole fusion process between each stage are formulated as follows: \begin{equation}v_i = V_i(f_{i-1}),\quad i \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\} f_i =  v_i + SGB_i(MMFB_i(v_i,\ l)),\ i \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}, I,\ i = 0 \end{equation} where \( V_i \) represents the \( i\text{-th \) stage of the Swin Transformer, and \( \text{SGB _i \) and \( \text{MMFB _i \) denote the \( i\text{-th \) fusion blocks placed between \( V_i \) and \( V_{i+1 \).}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}v_i = V_i(f_{i-1}),\quad i \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\} f_i =  v_i + SGB_i(MMFB_i(v_i,\ l)),\ i \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}, I,\ i = 0 \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-121: We describe the effects of the encoder fusion architecture in the ablation study in Table \ref{tab:tab4 .}
elements.sent-2411-122: Our decoder is designed to perform two tasks: classify the existence of the object specified by the text prompt and generate the corresponding segmentation mask.
elements.sent-2411-123: To accomplish this, we use a multi-scale deformable attention pixel decoder \cite{zhu2020deformable .}
elements.sent-2411-124: This decoder incorporates an FPN \cite{lin2017feature structure to effectively utilize multi-scale features, taking \( f_1, f_2, f_3, \) and \( f_4 \) as inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-125: The final segmentation mask \(M_c\) is produced to pass the output from the decoder through a \( 1 \times 1 \) convolution layer.
elements.sent-2411-126: In addition to mask generation, we introduce a parallel branch to compute the existence probability of the object described by the text prompt.
elements.sent-2411-127: This branch fuses the decoder output from \( f_4 \) with the raw language feature extracted from BERT using MHCA.
elements.sent-2411-128: This fusion enables the decoder to leverage both visual and textual information, enhancing its ability to determine the presence of the specified object.
elements.sent-2411-129: Unlike \cite{wu2024towards , which uses fused language features from the encoder for existence prediction, we use the raw language feature directly from BERT, as it retains richer semantic context.}
elements.sent-2411-130: After the fusion through MHCA, the existence probability \( p_c\) is computed by passing the output through a linear layer.
elements.sent-2411-131: Finally, we calculate the cross-entropy loss for both the generated mask and the existence probability to optimize the model’s accuracy in both segmentation and existence detection tasks, shown as follows: \begin{equation}L = BCELoss(p_c, y_c) + \lambda \cdot CELoss(M_c, M_c^{gt})\end{equation} where \(\lambda\) is a hyperparameter for mask loss, \(y_c\) and \(M_c^{gt}\) denote the ground truth of the existence and the mask of class \(c\), respectively.

elements.sent-2411-132: We propose an iterative inference strategy to use location prompts during inference, similar to those used in training.
elements.sent-2411-133: In the training phase, we can generate location prompts because we have access to the ground truth for the instrument, but this is not possible in the inference phase.
elements.sent-2411-134: Therefore, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:fig4 , we divide the inference process into two iterations.}
elements.sent-2411-135: In the first iteration, we generate the initial segmentation map and existence probability for each instrument category.
elements.sent-2411-136: We use two prompts: a category name prompt (e.g., “The monopolar curved scissors”) and a descriptive prompt generated by GPT, which provides visual characteristics of the instrument.
elements.sent-2411-137: For category \(c\), the model predicts the segmentation maps \(M_c^1 \text{ and M_c^2\) and the existence probabilities \(p_c^1 \text{ and p_c^2\).}}

elements.sent-2411-139: In the second iteration, spatial information is used to refine the segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-140: Based on the segmentation maps \(M_c^1 \text{ and M_c^2\) from the first iteration, the model calculates the center of mass for the detected object, allowing it to determine a rough location within one of four quadrants: left-top, left-bottom, right-top, or right-bottom.}
elements.sent-2411-141: This positional information is then incorporated into a new location prompt (e.g., “The monopolar curved scissors on the right bottom”), which serves as input for the second inference.

elements.sent-2411-143: For the final output \(M_c\), the segmentation maps from both iterations are combined using as below: \begin{equation}\label{eq:eq3} M_c=  0, if\ \frac{p_c^1 + p_c^2}{2} < 0.5 \frac{M_c^1 + M_c^2}{2}, \frac{p_c^1 + p_c^2}{2} \geq 0.5\ \  \ p_c^3 < 0.5 \frac{M_c^1 + M_c^2 + M_c^3}{3}, \frac{p_c^1 + p_c^2}{2} \geq 0.5\ \  \ p_c^3 \geq 0.5 \end{equation} This combination leverages both the fixed prompts and the location prompt, resulting in a more precise and contextually aware segmentation map.

elements.sent-2411-225: In this work, we presented Robust Surgical Instrument Segmentation (RoSIS), a novel framework focused on enhancing the robustness and effectiveness of text-promptable surgical instrument segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-226: Recognizing the limitations of existing methods, which often rely on assumptions about object presence, we redefined the task under robust conditions as Robust text-promptable SIS (R-SIS).
elements.sent-2411-227: RoSIS employs an encoder-decoder architecture with a Multi-Modal Fusion Block (MMFB) and a Selective Gate Block (SGB) to achieve a balanced integration of visual and language features, preventing the dominance of either modality.
elements.sent-2411-228: Additionally, we introduced an iterative inference strategy to refine segmentation maps, utilizing both name-based and location-based prompts to improve accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-229: Our experiments on the EndoVis2017 and EndoVis2018 datasets demonstrated that RoSIS outperforms existing vision-based and promptable methods under robust conditions.
elements.sent-2411-230: Ablation studies further validated the effectiveness of our fusion structures, as well as the iterative inference approach, confirming RoSIS's adaptability across diverse surgical scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-231: This work lays a foundation for future research in multi-modal surgical instrument segmentation, with applications in other areas where vision-language integration is essential for complex surgical scene understanding.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
elements.sent-2411-232: Surgical instrument segmentation (SIS) is essential in computer-assisted surgeries, with deep learning methods improving accuracy in complex environments.
elements.sent-2411-233: Recently, text-promptable segmentation methods have been introduced, generating masks based on textual descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-234: However, they assume the text-described object is present and always generate an associated mask even when the object is absent.
elements.sent-2411-235: Existing methods address this by using prompts only for objects already known to exist in the scene, which relies on inaccessible information.
elements.sent-2411-236: To address this, we rethink text-promptable SIS and redefine it under robust conditions as Robust text-promptable SIS (R-SIS).
elements.sent-2411-237: Unlike previous approaches, R-SIS is a process that analyzes text prompts for all surgical instrument categories without relying on external knowledge, identifies the instruments present in the scene, and segments them accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-238: Building on this, we propose Robust Surgical Instrument Segmentation (RoSIS), an optimized framework combining visual and language features for promptable segmentation in the R-SIS setting.
elements.sent-2411-239: RoSIS employs an encoder-decoder architecture with a Multi-Modal Fusion Block (MMFB) and a Selective Gate Block (SGB) for balanced integration of vision and language features.
elements.sent-2411-240: Additionally, an iterative refinement strategy enhances segmentation masks through a two-step process: an initial pass with name-based prompts, followed by refinement with location prompts.
elements.sent-2411-241: Experiments across multiple datasets and settings show that RoSIS outperforms existing vision-based and promptable segmentation methods under robust conditions.
elements.sent-2411-242: By rethinking text-promptable SIS, our work establishes a fair and effective approach to surgical instrument segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-243: Surgical Instrument Segmentation (SIS) \cite{shvets2018automatic is fundamental to enhancing surgical vision.}
elements.sent-2411-244: Deep learning-based SIS methods \cite{iglovikov2018ternausnet, jin2019incorporating, zhao2020learning, gonzalez2020isinet, baby2023forks, ayobi2023matis have shown promising results.}
elements.sent-2411-245: However, their performance is often limited by small datasets, and they lack adaptability for human-robot interaction in robot-assisted surgeries.
elements.sent-2411-246: To address these challenges, vision-language models have introduced text-promptable segmentation \cite{zhou2023text, yue2023part , inspired by Referring Image Segmentation (RIS) \cite{yang2022lavt, yu2018mattnet .}}
elements.sent-2411-247: RIS enables mask generation based on text descriptions, assuming the described object exists in the image.
elements.sent-2411-248: However, this assumption leads to incorrect masks when the object is absent.
elements.sent-2411-249: Generalized Referring Expression Segmentation \cite{liu2023gres and Robust Referring Image Segmentation \cite{wu2024towards tackle this by first verifying object existence before segmentation.}}
elements.sent-2411-250: A similar issue arises in text-promptable SIS methods.
elements.sent-2411-251: These methods generate segmentation masks based on textual descriptions, typically including the instrument’s name and additional attributes provided by large language models such as GPT-4 \cite{achiam2023gpt .}
elements.sent-2411-252: However, they also assume that the described instrument is present, leading to incorrect masks when it is absent.
elements.sent-2411-253: Existing approaches mitigate this by only generating prompts for instruments present in the image (inaccessible information, middle row of Fig \ref{fig:fig1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-254: However, this introduces inaccessible information during evaluation, resulting in unfair comparisons with vision-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-255: The presence or absence of an instrument is inaccessible in the real world without an additional detection process.
elements.sent-2411-256: Since such information is typically provided during training but not during evaluation or testing, using it for evaluation leads to unfair comparisons with vision-based methods that do not rely on such information.

This means they are led to settings without robust conditions; (3) Our robust promptable segmentation model uses prompts for all classes, generating segmentation maps for all categories under robust conditions.} \label{fig:fig1}

elements.sent-2411-257: To ensure fairness, we rethink text-promptable SIS and propose Robust text-promptable SIS (R-SIS).
elements.sent-2411-258: Unlike previous methods, R-SIS integrates text prompts for all dataset categories, regardless of their presence in the image.
elements.sent-2411-259: As illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:fig1 , previous promptable SIS methods \cite{zhou2023text, yue2024surgicalsam, yue2023part selectively use prompts only for objects detected in the image, relying on information unavailable to vision-based models.}}
elements.sent-2411-260: R-SIS eliminates this bias by incorporating negative prompts for absent objects and positive prompts for present ones, allowing the model to distinguish object presence during segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-261: We construct an R-SIS dataset using EndoVis2017 \cite{allan20192017 and EndoVis2018 \cite{allan20202018 , ensuring comprehensive prompts for robust training and evaluation.}}
elements.sent-2411-262: To validate our hypothesis, we measured the performance variations of text-promptable surgical instrument segmentation models in the previous setting (without a robust condition) and our R-SIS setting (with a robust condition).
elements.sent-2411-263: Conventional promptable SIS methods exhibit increased false positives under the R-SIS setting, leading to performance degradation, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:fig2 .}
elements.sent-2411-264: In some cases, these methods even perform worse than vision-based models that do not utilize language information.
elements.sent-2411-265: To overcome this limitation, we introduce Robust Surgical Instrument Segmentation (RoSIS), an optimized framework designed for robust conditions.
elements.sent-2411-266: RoSIS incorporates two key modules: the Multi-Modal Fusion Block (MMFB), which effectively integrates visual and textual features, and the Selective Gate Block (SGB), which dynamically balances visual and fused features.
elements.sent-2411-267: Additionally, we propose an iterative refinement strategy that enhances segmentation accuracy through a two-step process.
elements.sent-2411-268: The first step generates an initial segmentation using instrument name-based and large language model-based prompts.
elements.sent-2411-269: The second step refines the segmentation using location prompts derived from the initial prediction.
elements.sent-2411-270: Our experiments on EndoVis2017 and EndoVis2018 demonstrate that RoSIS outperforms existing vision-based and promptable segmentation methods under robust conditions.
elements.sent-2411-271: We carefully design our evaluation process to ensure fair benchmarking and validate the effectiveness of our RoSIS compared to existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-272: Our results demonstrate RoSIS’s robustness and reliability, reinforcing the advantages of our redefined R-SIS framework for surgical instrument segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-273: SIS involves predicting the pixel-wise regions of instruments in surgical images.
elements.sent-2411-274: Early research \cite{iglovikov2018ternausnet, gonzalez2020isinet, zhao2022trasetr, ayobi2023matis, baby2023forks applied image-based segmentation methods to SIS.}
elements.sent-2411-275: TernausNet \cite{iglovikov2018ternausnet introduced a modified U-Net \cite{ronneberger2015u architecture with pre-trained VGG11/VGG16 encoders and skip connections for precise location.}}
elements.sent-2411-276: ISINet \cite{gonzalez2020isinet , utilizing a Mask R-CNN \cite{he2017mask architecture, incorporates a temporal consistency module to refine instrument predictions across video frames.}}
elements.sent-2411-277: TraSeTR \cite{zhao2022trasetr employs a Track-to-Segment Transformer, combining query embeddings with contrastive learning for robust tracking and segmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-278: MATIS \cite{ayobi2023matis , a video-based SIS method, uses a two-stage structure with Mask2Former \cite{cheng2022masked to generate and classify region proposals, incorporating masked and deformable attention and video transformers for temporal consistency.}}
elements.sent-2411-279: S3Net \cite{baby2023forks extends Mask R-CNN \cite{he2017mask with a Multi-Scale Mask-Attended classifier, refining class predictions based on mask-attended features to improve instance segmentation.}}
elements.sent-2411-280: Recently, with advancements in segmentation techniques utilizing information beyond vision, promptable segmentation models incorporating external prompts, such as text or prototypes, have been proposed for SIS.
elements.sent-2411-281: SurgicalSAM \cite{yue2024surgicalsam introduces an efficient fine-tuning method for the Segment Anything Model (SAM) \cite{kirillov2023segment , which employs prompts (points, bounding boxes, or text inputs) from a prompt encoder.}}
elements.sent-2411-282: Since SAM was originally designed for natural domain image segmentation, SurgicalSAM adapts it for the surgical domain.
elements.sent-2411-283: Additionally, it introduces a prototype-based class prompt encoder that generates prompt embeddings for each class as inputs to SAM.
elements.sent-2411-284: Later, various text-promptable SIS methods were proposed, leveraging vision-language models for text-based segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-285: SP-SAM \cite{yue2023part builds upon SAM \cite{kirillov2023segment , incorporating both category-level and part-level text prompts.}}
elements.sent-2411-286: TP-SIS \cite{zhou2023text , based on CLIP \cite{radford2021learning , utilizes text prompts describing instruments, categorized into name-based prompts, CLIP’s prompt templates, and prompts generated from GPT-4 \cite{achiam2023gpt .}}}
elements.sent-2411-287: However, these methods assume that the described instrument is always present in the image, leading to incorrect masks when the object is absent.
elements.sent-2411-288: To mitigate this, existing approaches first identify the instruments and then generate prompts based on detected objects.

elements.sent-2411-290: We rethink text-promptable SIS by introducing Robust text-promptable SIS (R-SIS), which eliminates this assumption and ensures a fair evaluation.

elements.sent-2411-293: RIS aims to segment objects based on a given text expression.
elements.sent-2411-294: Early works like MAttNet \cite{yu2018mattnet used language attention networks and object proposals to match expressions with objects.}
elements.sent-2411-295: Later methods, such as VLT \cite{ding2021vision and EFN \cite{feng2021encoder , employed Bi-GRU language models and ResNet-based encoders to fuse multi-modal information.}}
elements.sent-2411-296: The adoption of Vision Transformers (ViT) \cite{dosovitskiy2020image has led to further advancements in RIS.}
elements.sent-2411-297: LAVT \cite{yang2022lavt integrates BERT-based text encoding with visual features from ViT through pixel-word attention, significantly improving segmentation accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-298: However, a key limitation of prior RIS models is the assumption that every text expression corresponds to an object in the image.
elements.sent-2411-299: This assumption leads to incorrect mask generation when no relevant object is present.
elements.sent-2411-300: Generalized Referring Expression Segmentation \cite{liu2023gres tackles this issue by introducing empty-target (no target) and multi-target scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-301: RefSegformer \cite{wu2024towards further refines this approach by explicitly handling the empty-target problem, ensuring robust segmentation even when the described object is absent.}
elements.sent-2411-302: This empty-target problem also affects text-promptable SIS methods, where prompts often refer to objects that do not exist in the image.
elements.sent-2411-303: Our work extends robust RIS framework to SIS, addressing false positives while ensuring fair comparisons between promptable and vision-based segmentation models.

elements.sent-2411-376: In this work, we redefined the task of text-promptable Surgical Instrument Segmentation (SIS) under robust conditions as Robust text-promptable SIS (R-SIS).
elements.sent-2411-377: Existing methods often assume the presence of objects described by prompts, leading to challenges in realistic surgical scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-378: Such assumptions introduce biases in evaluation, as they leverage prior object knowledge that is not available to vision-based models.
elements.sent-2411-379: R-SIS eliminates this dependency by enforcing prompt-based segmentation without prior knowledge of object presence.
elements.sent-2411-380: This ensures fairer comparisons between promptable and vision-based methods while improving robustness in real-world surgical applications.
elements.sent-2411-381: To optimize model performance for R-SIS, we introduce Robust Surgical Instrument Segmentation (RoSIS), a novel framework designed to enhance segmentation robustness and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-382: RoSIS employs an encoder-decoder architecture with MMFB and SGB to achieve a balanced integration of visual and language features, preventing the dominance of either modality.
elements.sent-2411-383: Additionally, we introduced an iterative refinement strategy to refine segmentation maps, utilizing both name-based and location-based prompts to improve accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-384: Ablation studies further validated the effectiveness of our fusion structures, as well as the iterative refinement approach, confirming RoSIS's adaptability across diverse surgical scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-385: This work lays a foundation for future research in multi-modal SIS, with applications in other areas where vision-language integration is essential for complex surgical scene understanding.. }
elements.sent-2411-386: Surgical instrument segmentation is an essential component of computer-assisted and robotic surgery systems.
elements.sent-2411-387: Vision-based segmentation models typically produce outputs limited to a predefined set of instrument categories, which restricts their applicability in interactive systems and robotic task automation.
elements.sent-2411-388: Promptable segmentation methods allow selective predictions based on textual prompts.
elements.sent-2411-389: However, they often rely on the assumption that the instruments present in the scene are already known, and prompts are generated accordingly, limiting their ability to generalize to unseen or dynamically emerging instruments.
elements.sent-2411-390: In practical surgical environments, where instrument existence information is not provided, this assumption does not hold consistently, resulting in false-positive segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-391: To address these limitations, we formulate a new task called Robust text-promptable Surgical Instrument Segmentation (R-SIS).
elements.sent-2411-392: Under this setting, prompts are issued for all candidate categories without access to instrument presence information.
elements.sent-2411-393: R-SIS requires distinguishing which prompts refer to visible instruments and generating masks only when such instruments are explicitly present in the scene.
elements.sent-2411-394: This setting reflects practical conditions where uncertainty in instrument presence is inherent.
elements.sent-2411-395: We evaluate existing segmentation methods under the R-SIS protocol using surgical video datasets and observe substantial false-positive predictions in the absence of ground-truth instruments.
elements.sent-2411-396: These findings demonstrate a mismatch between current evaluation protocols and real-world use cases, and support the need for benchmarks that explicitly account for prompt uncertainty and instrument absence.
elements.sent-2411-397: Minimally invasive surgeries \cite{fuchs2002minimally, harrell2005minimally are widely adopted due to their advantages, requiring surgeons to rely on camera-captured views instead of direct visualization.}
elements.sent-2411-398: Vision-based SIS models \cite{iglovikov2018ternausnet, jin2019incorporating, zhao2020learning, gonzalez2020isinet, baby2023forks, ayobi2023matis typically generate predictions for all predefined instrument categories, regardless of whether they are present in the surgical scene.}
elements.sent-2411-399: While effective in static settings, this behavior limits integration with interactive systems and automation pipelines, where selective and context-aware segmentation is often required.
elements.sent-2411-400: More recently, promptable segmentation approaches \cite{zhou2023text, yue2023part have been introduced, allowing segmentation to be guided by natural language descriptions.}
elements.sent-2411-401: Many recent promptable segmentation approaches are inspired by referring image segmentation (RIS) \cite{yang2022lavt, yu2018mattnet , which assumes that a query refers to an object that is present in the image.}
elements.sent-2411-402: RIS methods offer selective segmentation over target categories but commonly rely on the assumption that the prompted object is visible in the image.
elements.sent-2411-403: This assumption in RIS also applies to the promptable SIS method.
elements.sent-2411-404: However, this assumption does not consistently hold in real surgical environments, where we do not know the presence of instruments in the current scene (oracle information).
elements.sent-2411-405: This leads promptable segmentation models to often produce false-positive masks for absent instruments when prompted without presence verification \cite{wu2024towards .}
elements.sent-2411-406: We need a stricter standard in surgical environments due to the critical risks posed by false-positive masks when instrument presence is uncertain.
elements.sent-2411-407: Moreover, current evaluation protocols for promptable segmentation methods often rely on oracle information that is unavailable in practical scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-408: As illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:fig1 , these models \cite{zhou2023text, yue2024surgicalsam, yue2023part exclude prompts for absent instruments based on the ground truth, leading to evaluations that do not reflect real-world surgical environments.}}
elements.sent-2411-409: To address these limitations, we introduce a new task formulation: Robust text-promptable Surgical Instrument Segmentation (R-SIS).
elements.sent-2411-410: Under this setting, prompts are issued for all candidate categories without assuming knowledge of instrument presence.
elements.sent-2411-411: A model must determine whether the prompted instrument is visually present in the current scene and, only if confirmed, produce an appropriate segmentation mask.
elements.sent-2411-412: This process ensures that segmentation is based on visual features rather than relying on prior assumptions about instrument presence.
elements.sent-2411-413: This setting is also fit for real surgical systems, where the presence of instruments changes frequently in the camera's view.
elements.figure-2411-025: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figure1.pdf} \caption{Comparison of evaluation process of SIS method: (1) Vision-based segmentation model \cite{iglovikov2018ternausnet, jin2019incorporating, zhao2020learning, gonzalez2020isinet} only uses the image and generates all classes of segmentation maps; (2) Previous promptable segmentation model \cite{zhou2023text, yue2024surgicalsam, yue2023part} uses ``oracle information" to identify categories present in the image and generates segmentation maps only for those categories. This means they are led to settings without robust conditions; (3) Our R-SIS uses prompts for all classes, generating segmentation maps for all categories under robust conditions.} \label{fig:fig1}
elements.sent-2411-414: To support this task, we design a segmentation framework, referred to as Robust Surgical Instrument Segmentation (RoSIS), that integrates visual and textual features, predicts instrument existence, and generates segmentation outputs using multiple prompt types.
elements.sent-2411-415: The framework supports diverse prompt types and introduces iterative refinement to achieve improved segmentation performance under the R-SIS setting, without relying on prior knowledge of instrument presence.
elements.sent-2411-416: To evaluate segmentation performance under the R-SIS setting, we conduct experiments on two benchmark datasets, EndoVis2017 \cite{allan20192017 and EndoVis2018 \cite{allan20202018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-417: Through a series of detailed and diverse experiments, we demonstrate how prompt uncertainty and instrument absence affect segmentation behavior and highlight the importance of realistic evaluation protocols.
elements.sent-2411-418: In summary, our contributions are three-fold: (1) We define the R-SIS task, which evaluates promptable segmentation under realistic conditions without assuming instrument presence.
elements.figure-2411-026: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figure3.pdf} \caption{RoSIS model architecture with (a) overall structure, (b) Multi-Modal Fusion Block, and (c) Selective Gate Block.} \label{fig:fig3}
elements.sent-2411-419: However, these methods assume that the described instrument is always present in the image, leading to incorrect masks when the instrument is absent.
elements.sent-2411-420: To mitigate this, existing approaches first identify the instruments and then generate prompts based on detected instruments.
elements.sent-2411-421: This introduces oracle information during evaluation, leading to unfair comparisons with vision-only models.
elements.sent-2411-422: We rethink text-promptable SIS by introducing R-SIS, which eliminates this assumption and ensures a fair evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-423: Unlike prior methods that selectively use prompts for detected instruments, R-SIS systematically integrates prompts for all classes, allowing the model to infer instrument presence dynamically.
elements.sent-2411-424: Our approach also addresses the false positives inherent in previous text-promptable SIS models.
elements.sent-2411-425: This issue also affects text-promptable SIS methods, where prompts often refer to instruments that do not exist in the image.
elements.sent-2411-426: Our work extends the robust RIS framework to SIS, addressing false positives while ensuring fair comparisons between promptable and vision-based segmentation models.
elements.sent-2411-427: Section \ref{sec:sec3.3 describes the architecture of RoSIS, which is an encoder-decoder structure with multi-modal fusion components and an instrument existence prediction branch.}
elements.sent-2411-428: Finally, Section \ref{sec:sec3.4 introduces an iterative refinement strategy, applied during inference, that is designed to address the R-SIS task by determining the presence of instruments and leveraging diverse prompts to generate refined segmentation masks.}
elements.sent-2411-429: To enable R-SIS, we incorporate existence probabilities, allowing our model to assess the presence of each instrument class first.
elements.sent-2411-430: To do this, we calculate the center of mass for each instrument's ground truth mask to identify its typical position, creating prompts like ``The \{cls name\} on the \{location\}." During training, these location prompts are generated using ground truth annotations to ensure accurate positional information.
elements.sent-2411-431: For location prompts, we categorize positions into four options: ``left-top," ``left-bottom," ``right-top," and ``right-bottom." During model training, we employ all three types of text prompts—class name prompt, GPT-4 visual prompt, and location prompt—for each class \( c \), ensuring comprehensive contextual information.
elements.sent-2411-432: During inference, since ground truth is not accessible, we apply an iterative refinement strategy to dynamically estimate instrument locations and construct corresponding location prompts, thereby maximizing their utility under deployment conditions.
elements.sent-2411-433: If training only with positive prompts (describing instruments present in the image), the model may develop a bias toward the presence of instruments, potentially reducing generalization \cite{wu2024towards .}
elements.sent-2411-434: We design our Robust Surgical Instrument Segmentation (RoSIS) using an encoder-decoder structure to enable R-SIS.
elements.sent-2411-435: We describe the effects of the encoder fusion architecture in the ablation study in Table \ref{tab:tab3 .}
elements.sent-2411-436: Our decoder is designed to perform two tasks: classify the existence of the instrument specified by the text prompt and generate the corresponding segmentation mask.
elements.sent-2411-437: In addition to mask generation, we introduce a parallel branch to compute the existence probability of the instrument described by the text prompt.
elements.sent-2411-438: This fusion enables the decoder to leverage both visual and textual information, enhancing its ability to determine the presence of the specified instrument.
elements.sent-2411-439: Unlike \cite{wu2024towards , which uses fused language features from the encoder for existence prediction, we use raw BERT embeddings as input language features to preserve rich semantic context without introducing task-specific projection layers.}
elements.sent-2411-440: While prior work adopts cross-modal embeddings aligned during training, such methods often entangle vision and language representations, which may bias prompt behavior in the presence of instrument absence.
elements.figure-2411-027: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figure4.pdf} \caption{(a) Iterative refinement process of class $c$ (monopolar curved scissors in this image): (Iteration 1) Initial segmentation using a general prompt, (Iteration 2) refined segmentation with a location prompt referring to the first predicted map. Then, we combine maps by equation \ref{eq:eq3} for accurate final output. (b) After applying the iterative refinement process to all classes, the refined maps are combined to generate the final output.} \label{fig:fig4}
elements.sent-2411-441: We propose an iterative refinement strategy to use location prompts during inference.
elements.sent-2411-442: During training, given the ground-truth instruments present in the scene, RoSIS learns to predict segmentation masks by first determining the presence of instruments and utilizing diverse prompts, thereby reducing false positives under robust settings.
elements.sent-2411-443: During inference, where instrument existence information is not available, the model performs a two-iteration inference process, as illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:fig4 : it first predicts which instruments are visible by applying all instrument prompts, and then generates refined masks only for those instruments that are visually present in the scene.}
elements.sent-2411-444: In the second iteration, while location information can be directly obtained from the ground truth during training, it is not available during inference.
elements.sent-2411-445: Therefore, the model instead leverages the predicted masks from the first iteration to guide refinement.
elements.sent-2411-446: Based on the segmentation maps \(M_c^1 \text{ and M_c^2\) from the first iteration, the model calculates the center of mass for the detected instrument, allowing it to determine a rough location within one of four quadrants: left-top, left-bottom, right-top, or right-bottom.}
elements.sent-2411-447: We describe the effects of iterative refinement in detail in the ablation study in Table \ref{tab:tab4 .}

elements.sent-2411-458: We compare the performance of segmentation models under the R-SIS setting using false positive rate (FPR), precision (P), recall (R), and F1 score (F1).
elements.sent-2411-459: The results show that RoSIS substantially reduces the occurrence of false-positive predictions compared to prior promptable segmentation models.
elements.sent-2411-460: While existing methods often produce masks for absent instruments due to the assumption of instrument presence in prompts, RoSIS effectively suppresses these errors by incorporating an existence prediction mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-461: In addition to its low FPR, RoSIS achieves consistently high precision and recall, demonstrating that its robustness does not come at the cost of under-segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-462: These findings confirm the advantage of the R-SIS framework and support the use of prompt-agnostic prompting strategies for fair and reliable evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-463: Table~\ref{tab:model_fpr_precision_recall also introduces results for RoSIS without the existence prediction module (w/o exist), allowing direct comparison the effectiveness of this component.}
elements.sent-2411-464: The existence prediction module leads to a clear reduction in false positives and a noticeable gain in precision across both datasets, confirming its contribution to segmentation robustness.
elements.sent-2411-465: Notably, even without the existence prediction module, RoSIS outperforms prior promptable segmentation models, indicating that its core design, rooted in robust prompting strategies and R-SIS based task framing, is inherently better suited to the evaluation conditions.

elements.sent-2411-470: Existing methods often assume the presence of instruments described by prompts, leading to challenges in realistic surgical scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-471: Such assumptions introduce biases in evaluation, as they leverage prior knowledge that is not available to vision-based models.
elements.sent-2411-472: R-SIS eliminates this dependency by enforcing prompt-based segmentation without prior knowledge of instrument presence.
elements.sent-2411-473: To support this task setting, we developed Robust Surgical Instrument Segmentation (RoSIS), effective framework designed to address prompt uncertainty and instrument absence.
elements.sent-2411-474: Rather than relying on architectural complexity, RoSIS integrates prompt types with an existence prediction mechanism and an iterative refinement strategy, enabling reliable segmentation under R-SIS constraints.
elements.sent-2411-475: Our experiments on the EndoVis2017 and EndoVis2018 datasets demonstrate that RoSIS consistently reduces false positive predictions while maintaining strong precision and recall.
elements.sent-2411-476: Ablation studies further validate that even without existence prediction, RoSIS performs competitively against existing models, underscoring the value of our task-aware formulation.
elements.sent-2411-477: This work highlights the importance of fair evaluation protocols for promptable segmentation and provides a foundation for future research in multi-modal surgical scene understanding.
